file_name: Qamar et al. - 2021 - Dense encoder-decoder–based architecture for skin lesion segmentation.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning-based dense encoder-decoder architecture combining DenseNet and ResNet with Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) and dense skip connections to perform automatic skin lesion segmentation on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel dense encoder-decoder architecture combining DenseNet and ResNet for skin lesion segmentation. It details specific methods (ASPP, dense skip connections), implementation details (ISIC 2018 dataset, Keras/TensorFlow, hyperparameters), and reports original quantitative results (Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity) comparing the proposed model against baselines and state-of-the-art methods.

title: Dense Encoder-Decoder–Based Architecture for Skin Lesion Segmentation
brief_summary: This study proposes a dense encoder-decoder-based convolutional neural network architecture for automatic skin lesion segmentation to address challenges in melanoma diagnosis. The model combines DenseNet and ResNet features within a UNet-like framework, utilizing Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) for multi-scale context and dense skip connections to preserve fine-grained details. The approach was evaluated on the ISIC 2018 dataset using five-fold cross-validation with data augmentation techniques. Performance was measured using Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Specificity metrics. The proposed architecture achieved a Dice score of 90.00% and a Jaccard index of 83.30%, outperforming several state-of-the-art methods. The results demonstrate that combining DenseNet, ResNet, ASPP, and dense skip connections effectively improves segmentation accuracy for skin lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on automatic segmentation of skin lesions from dermoscopic images using the ISIC 2018 dataset. While melanoma is mentioned as the primary clinical motivation for accurate segmentation, the experiments treat the task as general skin lesion segmentation rather than classifying specific histopathological types.

Mentioned in the abstract and introduction as a dangerous, aggressive, and deadly skin cancer that necessitates early detection and accurate segmentation. It is not explicitly listed as a distinct segmentation class label in the experimental results, which focus on general lesion boundaries.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion boundaries/masks in dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The paper performs automatic skin lesion segmentation using a dense encoder-decoder architecture. The model predicts pixel-wise masks for skin lesions from the ISIC 2018 dataset. Performance is evaluated using segmentation metrics such as Dice index, Jaccard index, Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions, used for evaluating the proposed skin lesion segmentation architecture.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity) and training times without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2018.evaluation_setting: Comparison of proposed architecture against ablation variations and state-of-the-art methods
ISIC 2018.notes: The proposed architecture achieved the highest Dice score (90.00%), Jaccard index (83.30%), Accuracy (96.95%), and Sensitivity (96.50%) among the compared methods. It also achieved the highest Thresholded Jaccard Index (80.1%). The dilated model required more training time (21h 30min) than the non-dilated model (17h 45min) but achieved higher accuracy. The proposed model had fewer parameters (46.70M) than the Residual-based ablation (88.50M).

architecture_used.main_model: Dense Encoder-Decoder-based CNN (Combining DenseNet and ResNet with ASPP and Dense Skip Connections)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; paper notes that existing methods used non-biomedical features which were not ideal, implying the proposed method may not rely on standard ImageNet pretraining or uses features specifically adapted for skin lesions, but specific pretraining weights are not detailed.

conclusions: The proposed dense encoder-decoder architecture, which combines DenseNet and ResNet with Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) and dense skip connections, achieves superior skin lesion segmentation performance compared to ablation variations (ResNet-only and DenseNet-only) and state-of-the-art methods.
The proposed model achieved a Dice score of 90.00%, Jaccard index of 83.30%, Accuracy of 96.95%, Sensitivity of 96.50%, and Specificity of 97.00% on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

The combination of DenseNet and ResNet effectively preserves contextual information in the encoder-decoder paths and recovers fine-grained details through dense skip connections, addressing limitations of non-biomedical pre-trained features and lack of multi-scale information in previous methods.

The dilated model required more training time (21 hours 30 minutes) than the non-dilated model (17 hours 45 minutes) but achieved significantly higher accuracy (96.95% vs. 85.78%), indicating that the computational overhead of dilation is justified by performance gains.
The proposed architecture has fewer parameters (46.70M) than the Residual-based ablation model (88.50M), demonstrating efficiency despite its complexity.

The authors acknowledge that while the method is effective for skin lesion segmentation, it is intended to be helpful for other medical image segmentation tasks in the future, implying current validation is limited to this specific domain.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single dataset (ISIC 2018) with no external validation on independent cohorts, limiting generalizability.
The authors acknowledge that the small size of the training dataset (2594 images) prevents full utilization of the deep network's feature representation capabilities.
The model exhibits failure cases (low contrast between classes) where it produces larger false-negative regions, particularly when skin color is similar to the lesion area.
The reported specificity (97.00%) is lower than some state-of-the-art methods, which the authors attribute to limited GPU memory constraints during training.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance comparisons rely solely on descriptive metrics.
The paper does not report standard deviations or confidence intervals for the reported metrics, only single-point estimates from five-fold cross-validation.
The model relies on non-biomedical pre-trained features (implied by the critique of existing methods and lack of specific biomedical pretraining details), which may limit feature relevance.
Training time was significantly longer for the dilated model (21h 30min) compared to the non-dilated model (17h 45min), indicating computational overhead.
The study focuses on general skin lesion segmentation rather than specific histopathological types (e.g., melanoma vs. benign), limiting clinical specificity assessment.
